---
title: "Is It Real, or Just Luck?"
subtitle: "Data, pictures, and the t-test, for NeuroCamp"
author: "DataSquad · UCLA Data Science Center"
output-file: index.html
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    theme: cosmo
    code-tools: false
engine: knitr
filters:
  - webr
webr:
  packages: ['ggplot2', 'dplyr', 'infer']
  cell-options:
    autorun: false
---

> **For students:** Run a code cell by clicking the **Run Code** button under it.
> You can change the numbers and run it again. You can't break anything, so try stuff!
>
> The very first cell takes a little while (your browser is downloading R).
> After that it's fast.

# 1. Our question

This is data from a **real experiment.** Researchers gave 18 sheep heart attacks, then
treated half with **embryonic stem cells** (`esc`) and left the other half untreated
(`ctrl`). They measured each heart's **pumping capacity** (the percent of blood the heart
pushes out per beat) **before** and **after**.

**Can stem cells help a damaged heart recover?**

Let's look at the data as a table, like a spreadsheet:

```{webr-r}
# Real data from a sheep stem-cell study (pumping capacity is a percent, 0-100)
sheep <- read.csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/jt14den/neurocamp-stats-viz/main/data/stem-cell.csv")
sheep
```

Each **row** is one sheep. The **columns** are its treatment group and its heart pumping
capacity before and after. That's all a dataset is: rows of subjects, columns of what we
measured.

Notice the numbers aren't all the same. That **variation** is what this whole lesson is
about: where does it come from, and can the treatment explain some of it?

::: callout-note
## The same five steps, in any tool
Almost every data project runs the same loop, whether you use R, Python, or a spreadsheet:

1. **Get the data in** (load / import)
2. **Clean it** (fix labels, drop bad values)
3. **Restructure it** (make new columns, or reshape / pivot between "wide" and "long")
4. **Summarize it** (averages, counts, by group)
5. **Analyze it** (charts, tests)

We're using R today, but the *thinking* transfers straight to Excel or Google Sheets.
Where it helps, we'll show the spreadsheet version of a step too.
:::

::: {.panel-tabset}
## Why use code today
- It **remembers every step**, so anyone can re-run and check your work (reproducible).
- It makes the **shuffle simulation** in section 5 easy. A spreadsheet really can't.
- With webr it runs in any browser, with **nothing to install**.

## Where a spreadsheet is great
- A quick look at small data, or a fast chart.
- You may already know it, and the **concepts are identical** (clean, new columns, pivot, chart).
- Totally fine for simple averages and graphs when you don't need to reproduce or simulate.
:::

# 2. Real data is messy, so clean it first

Real experiments never hand you tidy data. Here's a messier version of the same study.
Load it and see if you can spot the problems:

```{webr-r}
messy <- read.csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/jt14den/neurocamp-stats-viz/main/data/stem-cell-messy.csv")
messy
```

Three things are wrong. Can you find them?

1. A **blank** value (missing data).
2. An **impossible value**: an `after` of 425. Pumping capacity is a percent, so it can't
   go above 100. Someone fumbled a decimal (42.5 became 425).
3. The labels don't match: `Ctrl`, `ctrl `, `esc`, `ESC`, `Esc`. To the computer those
   are all different groups.

Let's clean it:

```{webr-r}
library(dplyr)

clean <- messy |>
  mutate(trmt = tolower(trimws(trmt))) |>   # fix labels: "Control"/"ctrl " -> "ctrl"
  filter(!is.na(after)) |>                  # drop the blank row
  filter(after <= 100)                      # drop the impossible value

clean
```

**Why this matters:** one value of 425 instead of 42.5 would wreck every average and chart
that follows. Cleaning is not busywork; it's part of doing the science right.

::: callout-tip
## Same idea in a spreadsheet
The exact same operations: **Find & Replace** to fix `ESC`/`Esc` into `esc`, then **filter or
sort** to spot the blank and the 425 and delete those rows. Same moves, different buttons.
This "clean before you analyze" step happens in *every* tool.
:::

# 3. Build the number we actually care about

The question is about **recovery**, so what matters is how much each heart *changed*:

```{webr-r}
sheep <- sheep |>
  mutate(change = after - before)   # positive = the heart got stronger
sheep
```

::: callout-tip
## Same idea in a spreadsheet
You'd add a new column with `=after - before` and fill it down. Building a **calculated
column** is one of the most common data moves there is, in any tool. Its cousin is
**reshaping** or **pivoting**, switching data between "wide" and "long". Our `before`/`after`
columns are "wide"; you'd pivot them "long" if you wanted to chart the change over time.
:::

Now summarize each group: the typical change (average) and how much the sheep vary (spread).

::: callout-tip
## Predict first
Before you run it: which group do you think improved more, and roughly by how much? Write
down a number, then check.
:::

```{webr-r}
sheep |>
  group_by(trmt) |>
  summarise(avg_change = mean(change),
            spread     = sd(change),
            n = n())
```

The **average is our best single guess** for a group: if you had to predict one sheep's
change knowing only its group, you'd guess that group's average. The **spread is the
leftover** the average misses, how much real sheep bounce around that guess.

The stem-cell group's average change is positive (hearts improved), the control group's is
negative (hearts got worse). But both groups have spread, so individuals overlap. Hold that
thought.

::: callout-tip
## Same idea in a spreadsheet
This grouped summary is exactly what a **PivotTable** does: drag treatment to the rows, drag
change to the values, set it to "Average." `group_by()` + `summarise()` is the PivotTable of
the code world.
:::

# 4. Picture it: choosing and making a graph

Always look at your data before trusting a single number.

Which graph? Match the chart to the question:

- comparing **groups of numbers** → dot plot or box plot
- one number **over time** → line chart
- **two numbers** against each other → scatter plot

::: callout-tip
## Predict first
Picture the plot before it appears: where do the stem-cell dots sit compared to the control
dots, above or below the line at 0? Do they overlap?
:::

Here every dot is one sheep's change:

```{webr-r}
library(ggplot2)

ggplot(sheep, aes(x = trmt, y = change, color = trmt)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.08, size = 3, alpha = 0.8) +
  labs(x = "Treatment group", y = "Change in pumping capacity (%)",
       title = "Every sheep, one dot (above 0 = improved)") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 16) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")
```

Add a box to show the middle 50% of each group:

```{webr-r}
ggplot(sheep, aes(x = trmt, y = change, fill = trmt)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.4, outlier.shape = NA) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.08, size = 3, alpha = 0.8) +
  labs(x = "Treatment group", y = "Change in pumping capacity (%)",
       title = "Boxes show the middle 50% of each group") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 16) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")
```

**Talk about it:** the stem-cell group improved more, but the groups *overlap*. Some
control sheep did better than some treated sheep.

# 5. Is it real, or just luck?

The stem-cell group changed more on average. **But does that prove stem cells work?**

If we split these 18 sheep into two random groups by coin flip, the two averages still
wouldn't be *exactly* equal. Random groups differ a little, by luck alone. So we need to
tell a **real** difference from a **lucky** one.

**The trick:** if the treatment did nothing, the labels `esc` and `ctrl` would be
meaningless, and we could **shuffle** them and get a difference just as big. So let's
shuffle 1000 times and see what luck produces.

```{webr-r}
library(infer)

# Our real difference (stem-cell average change minus control average change)
real_diff <- sheep |>
  specify(change ~ trmt) |>
  calculate(stat = "diff in means", order = c("esc", "ctrl"))

real_diff
```

::: callout-tip
## Predict first
Make the call now, before the computer shows you: is this difference **real**, or could
**luck** alone explain it? Commit to an answer.
:::

```{webr-r}
# Shuffle the labels 1000 times = "what does luck look like?"
luck <- sheep |>
  specify(change ~ trmt) |>
  hypothesize(null = "independence") |>
  generate(reps = 1000, type = "permute") |>
  calculate(stat = "diff in means", order = c("esc", "ctrl"))

visualize(luck) +
  shade_p_value(obs_stat = real_diff, direction = "greater") +
  labs(title = "Differences you get by pure luck",
       subtitle = "The red line is our real result") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 15)
```

**Read the picture:** if the red line is way out past the pile of "luck" differences, then
luck almost never makes a gap this big, so the difference is probably **real**. If the red
line sits inside the pile, luck makes gaps like this all the time, and we can't claim
stem cells helped.

::: callout-important
## Why a coding tool instead of a spreadsheet?
You *could* do most of the earlier steps in Excel. But this one, shuffling the labels 1000
times and rebuilding the picture, is painful by hand and nearly impossible to do cleanly with
clicks. Code also **remembers every step**, so anyone can re-run and check your work. That
reproducibility, plus simulations like this, is the real reason scientists reach for code.
:::

That shaded area has a name: the **p-value**. The shortcut scientists use instead of
shuffling is the **t-test**:

```{webr-r}
t.test(change ~ trmt, data = sheep)
```

Find the **p-value** in the output.

- **Small p-value (less than 0.05):** hard to explain by luck → probably real.
- **Big p-value:** luck could easily do this → we can't claim a real difference.

::: callout-important
## Two honest warnings (real scientists get these wrong!)
1. A small p-value does **not** mean the effect is "big" or "important." Only "probably
   not luck." The size of the change (a few percent) is a separate question.
2. A p-value is **not** "the chance I'm wrong." It's "how often pure luck would fool me."
:::

# 6. Your turn

::: callout-note
## Wait, where is R running?
There's **nothing to install** and **no cloud account**. This page *is* R: a tool called
webr runs R right inside your browser tab. Bookmark this link and it still works later. One
catch: if you **refresh the page it resets**, so copy out any code or results you want to keep.
:::

Same logic works for **your NeuroCamp experiments**. Did your habituated *C. elegans*
respond differently from the controls? Did the treated earthworms differ? Whenever you
compare two groups, the recipe is the same:

1. **Get the data in** and **clean it.**
2. **Build the measure** you care about, then **summarize** it (average + spread).
3. **Plot it** (dots + box). Do the groups overlap a lot, or barely?
4. **Test it:** is the difference real, or just luck?

**Try it with your own numbers.** Type your two groups into the cell below (replace the
example, keep the quotes and commas), then run it:

```{webr-r}
mydata <- data.frame(
  group = c("control", "control", "control", "treated", "treated", "treated"),
  value = c(      12,        15,        11,        19,        22,        18)
)
mydata
```

Now run the same moves on your data, one cell at a time:

```{webr-r}
# summarize: average and spread per group
library(dplyr)
mydata |> group_by(group) |> summarise(avg = mean(value), spread = sd(value), n = n())
```

```{webr-r}
# plot it
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(mydata, aes(x = group, y = value, color = group)) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.08, size = 3) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 16) + theme(legend.position = "none")
```

```{webr-r}
# is it real, or luck?
t.test(value ~ group, data = mydata)
```

Got a lot of data, or want to keep your work? Two easy options: come back to this page and
re-type it later, or do the **same steps in a spreadsheet** (clean, calculated column,
PivotTable, chart, `T.TEST`) using the "Same idea in a spreadsheet" notes above.

# 7. Going further (finished early? want a harder problem?)

These go past the basics. **Try each one before opening the solution.** The earlier cells
(`sheep`, `real_diff`, `luck`) are still loaded, so you can build on them.

### Challenge 1 — How *big* is the effect, really?

A small p-value says "probably real," not "big." Find the actual size of the difference,
then divide it by the typical spread to get an **effect size**. Is this a big effect, or a
tiny one that we only noticed because the data was clean?

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Solution"}
```{webr-r}
m <- sheep |> dplyr::group_by(trmt) |> dplyr::summarise(avg = mean(change), s = sd(change))
diff      <- m$avg[m$trmt == "esc"] - m$avg[m$trmt == "ctrl"]
pooled_sd <- sqrt(mean(m$s^2))
c(difference = round(diff, 2), effect_size = round(diff / pooled_sd, 2))
```

The difference is about **8 percentage points**, and the effect size is close to **2**, which
means the gap is almost *two whole spreads* wide. That's a **large** effect, not just a
barely-detectable one. Always report the size, not only the p-value.
:::

### Challenge 2 — Did the two groups start out the same?

A fair experiment needs the groups to be similar *before* treatment, otherwise one had a
head start. Check whether the `before` values were comparable across groups. Why does this
matter for trusting the result?

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Solution"}
```{webr-r}
sheep |> dplyr::group_by(trmt) |> dplyr::summarise(avg_before = mean(before))
t.test(before ~ trmt, data = sheep)$p.value
```

The baseline averages are close and the p-value is large, so there's **no real difference
at the start**. Random assignment did its job. That's what lets us credit the *after*
difference to the stem cells rather than to a head start.
:::

### Challenge 3 — Compute the p-value yourself

The shaded area in section 5 *is* the p-value. Instead of reading it off the plot, **count
it**: what fraction of the 1000 shuffles produced a difference at least as big as the real
one?

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Solution"}
```{webr-r}
mean(luck$stat >= real_diff$stat)
```

That proportion is the p-value, built from scratch. It should land close to the t-test's
p-value. The p-value was never magic, it's just "how often luck matched or beat us."
:::

### Challenge 4 — Does more data change the story?

Pretend we ran the same study with **three times as many sheep** by stacking the data on
itself. The averages and spread stay identical. Predict what happens to the p-value, then
check.

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Solution"}
```{webr-r}
big <- dplyr::bind_rows(sheep, sheep, sheep)   # same pattern, 3x the rows
t.test(change ~ trmt, data = big)$p.value
```

The p-value gets **much smaller**, even though the difference and the spread didn't change at
all. **p-values depend on sample size:** with enough data, even a tiny difference becomes
"statistically significant." This is exactly why Challenge 1 (effect size) matters.
:::

### Challenge 5 — Be a skeptic: is it driven by one weird sheep?

The mean is sensitive to outliers. Redo the shuffle test using the **median** difference
instead of the mean. If the result still holds, it isn't being carried by one unusual sheep.

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Solution"}
```{webr-r}
library(infer)
real_med <- sheep |> specify(change ~ trmt) |>
  calculate(stat = "diff in medians", order = c("esc", "ctrl"))

luck_med <- sheep |> specify(change ~ trmt) |>
  hypothesize(null = "independence") |>
  generate(reps = 1000, type = "permute") |>
  calculate(stat = "diff in medians", order = c("esc", "ctrl"))

mean(luck_med$stat >= real_med$stat)
```

If this p-value is also small, the conclusion is **robust**: it survives switching from the
mean to the median, so it's not an artifact of one extreme value. Checking whether a result
holds up under a different choice is real scientific thinking.
:::

---

*Real data: a randomized stem-cell trial in sheep (Menasché et al., via the openintro R
package). Questions about your data? The DataSquad and the UCLA Data Science Center help
students with exactly this.*
